summary:
The Spanish Founding of Manila (1571 AD) established Spain's Asian colonial capital and created one of the world's first genuinely global trade hubs — a city that connected Spanish America's silver with Chinese silk, porcelain, and luxury goods through the [[event:2426|Manila Galleon trade]] route. Miguel López de Legazpi, who had arrived in the Philippines in 1565, defeated the local Muslim ruler and established Manila on the southern shore of Manila Bay — a superb natural harbor that became Spain's gateway to Asia. The Philippines, named for [[event:2498|Philip II of Spain]], became the only Spanish colony in Asia, creating a unique outpost of Hispanic culture in the Far East.

Manila's significance derived primarily from its position as the Pacific terminus of the galleon trade: silver from Mexican mines at [[event:2425|Potosí]] and New Spain was shipped across the Pacific to Manila, where it was exchanged for Chinese goods brought by merchants through the [[event:3244|Canton System]]. Chinese traders (called 'Sangleys' by the Spanish) flocked to Manila in such numbers that they soon vastly outnumbered the Spanish population — creating tensions that erupted periodically in Spanish massacres of the Chinese community (major massacres occurred in 1603, 1639, and 1662). Despite these atrocities, Chinese merchants returned each time because Manila trade was too profitable to abandon. The Spanish Philippines, though economically dependent on the galleon trade, also became an important center of Catholic missionary activity throughout Asia, with Franciscan, Dominican, and Augustinian friars using Manila as a base for missions to Japan, China, and mainland Southeast Asia.
